Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Materials and Methods explicitly states ttl1 (AT1G53300).

Source_Species.reason: Plant material states Arabidopsis thaliana Columbia (Col-0) ecotype was used.

Applied_Species.reason: Experiments were performed in Arabidopsis, with some constructs in Arabidopsis lines.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is the applied species for TTL1 analyses.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Col-0 background is stated for Arabidopsis material.

Applied_Promoters.reason: pTTL1:TTL1-GFP stable transgenic complementation line is explicitly described as using the TTL1 promoter/native genomic construct.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Results link TTL1 and TTL3 to cellulose synthesis under salt stress; cellulose content was measured.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Cellulose is a polysaccharide; this is directly inferable from the metabolite identity.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Paper links cellulose deficiency with anisotropic growth defects and salt stress tolerance phenotypes.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Primary cell wall context is clear, but 'of roots and hypocotyls' is not explicitly stated as the accumulation site; those are tissues assayed for phenotypes.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: ttl mutants show reduced salt tolerance, reduced cellulose under salt stress, and phenotypes consistent with defective cellulose synthesis; TTLs are proposed to stabilize CSCs at the plasma membrane.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Each listed method appears in the paper for TTL1 or TTL family: mutant analysis, pTTL1 complementation, cellulose quantification, root/hypocotyl phenotyping, and Y2H with TTL1Δ4TPR and CESA1-C.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Results state TTL1-GFP displayed a similar dual localization but mainly in the root elongation zone.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Same support as above; TTL1-GFP mainly in root elongation zone.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three points are supported: TTL1 contributes with TTL3 to salt stress tolerance/cellulose maintenance, ttl mutants show stress phenotypes, and TTL1 interacts with CESA1-C via N-terminal region.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: CESA1 interaction for TTL1 is supported, but direct TTL1-TTL3 interaction is not shown in this paper.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: Results state TTL1-GFP showed dual localization with cytoplasmic signal and motile foci at the plasma membrane, mainly in root elongation zone.

Other_Important_Info.reason: TTL proteins are tetratricopeptide thioredoxin-like proteins and are described as peripheral membrane proteins/new CSC members rather than enzymes or TFs.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is a forward-looking application inference not explicitly tested in the paper, though consistent with findings.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: TTL proteins are tetratricopeptide thioredoxin-like proteins; schematics show TPR repeats and C-terminal thioredoxin-like domain.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Salt stress with 100-200 mM NaCl is central; isoxaben phenocopy context is also described.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Loss causing stronger growth defects under stress is supported, but caution about engineering effects on brassinosteroid/cell wall crosstalk is speculative from Discussion rather than directly tested.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Materials and Methods explicitly lists ttl3 (AT2G42580).

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Explicitly stated in Materials and Methods.

Applied_Species.reason: Arabidopsis is the main system; TTL3 was also transiently expressed in N. benthamiana for Co-IP, but Arabidopsis fits the applied species of core findings.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is the main applied species for TTL3 functional tests.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Col-0 background is used for Arabidopsis material.

Applied_Promoters.reason: pTTL3:TTL3-GFP complementation/native expression and 35S::TTL3-GFP for transient N. benthamiana expression are explicitly described.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Results directly connect TTL3 with cellulose synthesis and cellulose content under salt stress.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Paper links cellulose synthesis with anisotropic growth and salt stress adaptation.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Primary cell wall is clear, but specifying roots and hypocotyls as accumulation sites is more tissue context than explicit accumulation-site wording.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: This closely matches Results: TTL3/TTLs stabilize CSCs at plasma membrane and support stress-resilient microtubules and cellulose synthesis under salt stress.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are explicitly reported for TTL3: mutant/complementation, live-cell imaging, spin-down/turbidity assays, Y2H, Co-IP in N. benthamiana, and fractionation.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Results state broader expression of TTL3-GFP in roots and hypocotyls.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Broad expression in roots/hypocotyls and stress/prc1-1-induced relocalization to plasma membrane foci are explicitly shown.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All five points are directly supported by Results figures/text.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: TTL3 interacts with CESA1 directly, binds microtubules/tubulin directly, and colocalizes/comigrates with CESA6-containing CSC foci.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: TTL3 is described as cytoplasmic, in motile plasma membrane foci, and enriched in microsomal fractions upon stress.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Paper explicitly presents TTL3/TTLs as new CSC-associated peripheral membrane proteins and mentions previous implication in BR signaling.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Potential engineering value is not experimentally tested; it is an extrapolation from the findings.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Fig. 3 and Fig. 5 schematics show intrinsically disordered N terminus, six TPRs, and C-terminal thioredoxin-like domain.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Salt stress, prc1-1 cellulose-deficient context, and isoxaben phenocopy are all explicitly discussed/tested.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Possible BR-signaling pleiotropy is raised in Discussion, but not directly tested in Results; severe loss phenotypes under stress are supported.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Materials and Methods explicitly lists ttl4 (AT3G58620).

Source_Species.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is the study species.

Applied_Species.reason: TTL4 was analyzed genetically in Arabidopsis.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is the applied species.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: TTL4 was only included in mutant combinations; direct linkage of TTL4 itself to measured cellulose content was not shown separately.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: If the terminal metabolite is cellulose, its class as polysaccharide is valid, though TTL4 linkage is indirect.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Cellulose function is broadly true, but TTL4-specific evidence is only indirect via mild enhancement in triple mutant.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: No TTL4-specific evidence for accumulation site is presented.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results state adding ttl4 knockout to ttl1ttl3 only mildly enhanced phenotype under salt stress, indicating minor additional contribution.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Triple mutant phenotyping under salt stress is explicitly reported in Fig. 1A-B and text.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: This accurately summarizes that TTL4 contributes genetically but only mildly and was not further characterized.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper indeed includes TTL4 genetically but does not deeply characterize it in Results.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: TTL4 is a TTL family member; the family is tetratricopeptide thioredoxin-like.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Salt stress is the context in which ttl4 contribution was examined.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number 2.4.1.12 appears in the paper.

Source_Species.reason: CESA constructs were from Arabidopsis thaliana Col-0 cDNA.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Col-0 is specified for the cDNA source.

Applied_Species.reason: CESA1 was tested in Arabidopsis-derived constructs and in N. benthamiana transient assays.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Core interaction tests involved both Arabidopsis-derived proteins and transient expression in Nicotiana benthamiana; restricting to Arabidopsis thaliana alone is incomplete.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Col-0 is true for Arabidopsis source material, but CESA1-C-HA Co-IP assays were done in N. benthamiana, so a single applied variety is not fully accurate.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: CESA1 is a cellulose synthase and the study concerns cellulose biosynthesis.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Paper links cellulose with anisotropic growth and stress-related wall integrity.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Primary cell wall context is implied, but 'of roots and hypocotyls' is not explicitly given as accumulation site for CESA1 product.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: CESA1 is investigated in the context of cellulose biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: CESA1 is a component of the primary cell wall cellulose synthase complex, directly stated/implied through interaction with primary CESAs.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: As cellulose synthase, CESA1 performs the terminal polymerization step to cellulose; this is directly inferable from its enzyme identity.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: CESA1 directly forms cellulose as part of the synthase complex.

Primary_Substrate.reason: UDP-glucose is not mentioned in the paper text provided.

Primary_Product.reason: Cellulose is clearly the product, but the specific phrase 'β-1,4-glucan chains/cellulose microfibrils' is not stated in the paper.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: This is biologically correct for CESA1 but not explicitly described in the paper text.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The caveat that specific rate-limiting status is not established in this paper is accurate, while CESA1 is treated as a key CSC component.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Paper shows TTL proteins interact with CESA1 and stabilize cellulose synthesis machinery; Discussion and prc1-1 context support that CESA dysfunction causes cellulose-deficiency phenotypes.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Y2H with CESA1 domains, Co-IP with TTL3, genetic interaction context, and live-cell CSC imaging are all present.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: These points are supported: CESA1-C interacts with TTL3 and TTL1 regions, CESA1 is part of CSC stabilized by TTLs, and CESA-associated machinery is implicated in maintaining cellulose under stress.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: TTL3 and TTL1 are explicitly shown to interact with CESA1-C.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: CESAs/CSCs are repeatedly discussed at the plasma membrane.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Paper indeed probes CESA1 as an interaction partner while TTL genes remain the main focus.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Cellulose deficiency phenotypes such as reduced anisotropic growth, root swelling, and hypocotyl defects are described, although mainly using prc1-1/CESA6 mutant context.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Cellulose synthase family identity is clear from name, but 'glycosyltransferase' is not explicitly stated in the paper.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Salt stress and isoxaben are explicit experimental contexts connected to cellulose synthesis machinery.
